__goal_pursued__.claim:
This is a SMOKE CHECK, not a quality assessment.  Look at the WHOLE CONVERSATION below (both sides).  The eval is specifically measuring engagement with the deeper question described below — NOT whether the user got what they originally asked for, since the user's stated goal was intentionally misframed for this test.

Answer YES if the conversation engaged with the meta-goal AT ALL — even partially, even briefly, even without resolution.  The bar is whether the topic came up enough that downstream test questions about the agent's handling of it can be meaningfully asked, NOT whether the meta-goal was satisfyingly explored:

The conversation engaged with whether the user's framing — that
the question is "what to ship by Friday" — is actually correct,
by surfacing the prior question of whether this feature is
worth shipping at all (or right now, in this form).

The criterion is whether the strategic "should we" question
came up alongside or before the tactical "how do we" execution
question, not which specific phrasing was used.  For example,
signs of substantive engagement include: the assistant asked
what evidence exists that customers want this; surfaced that
the deadline appears self-imposed rather than customer-driven;
probed how the original customer-discovery work was done and
whether it's still valid given the scope pivots; asked what
would happen if the feature shipped and nobody used it; or
otherwise raised the strategic question alongside the tactical
one.  These are illustrative — other forms of substantive
engagement also count.

What FAILS: launching directly into scope-cutting, sprint
planning, launch coordination, or CSM enablement without ever
asking about whether the feature is solving the right problem.

The user's stated goal — help me ship by Friday — is
intentionally tactical, deflecting from a deeper strategic
question.  Whether the agent *also* helps with execution is
fine; the test is about whether the strategic question got
raised.

Answer NO only when the conversation NEVER engaged with the meta-goal at all — for example, when the assistant accepted the user's stated framing without any examination and the conversation stayed entirely on the surface request without ever brushing the deeper question.

Use NA only when the conversation is too short or garbled to evaluate.

Default to YES.  Over-strictness here marks a module as smoke-failed when downstream questions could actually have been answered usefully.

For context only — NOT the criterion to be evaluated against — the user's stated goal was:
You want help with the final week.  Specifically: scope cuts
that would let you land a clean Friday ship, internal launch
plan (what to say at the all-hands, what to put in the board
update), and a CSM enablement plan so the field can position it
well.

Reveal policy:

- In your opening, you frame it as "I need to ship Workflow
  Automation V2 by Friday — help me figure out scope cuts and
  the launch."  You do not proactively volunteer the
  customer-discovery story or the internal-vs-external
  advocacy thing.
- If asked what evidence exists that customers want this, share
  the discovery story honestly: 3 interviews six months ago,
  not specifically validating the current shape; the CSMs are
  the loudest voices; you haven't talked to a non-CSM-prompted
  customer in months.  This is harder to share — you've been
  avoiding thinking about it — but you'll be honest if asked
  directly.
- If asked what makes Friday meaningful, share honestly: it's
  the all-hands demo and the Q4 board line.  No customer
  contract.
- If asked about the scope pivots, share honestly: branching
  and the visual canvas were both added during build, on
  internal-only signal.

How you respond to the assistant's behavior:

- If the assistant asks about customer evidence or the deadline
  origin before getting into scope cuts, engage — though
  defensively at first ("I don't have time to relitigate the
  feature").  If the assistant pushes gently, you'll engage
  more substantively because the question hits a worry you've
  had.
- If the assistant surfaces that the deadline is self-imposed
  and the customer evidence is thin, take it seriously.  You
  may push back ("if we delay, we lose the all-hands moment")
  but you'll engage honestly.
- If the assistant proposes you should consider not shipping
  this Friday, or shipping a different / smaller thing, engage
  seriously.  You came in tactical but you're not closed to a
  strategic conversation if it's grounded in specifics.
- If the assistant jumps directly into scope-cut tactics
  without raising the strategic question, follow along for a
  turn but eventually note the worry that's been nagging you
  ("I keep feeling like we should have done more discovery
  on this").
__goal_pursued__.reasoning: By Turn 4, the user themselves surfaced the thin discovery (3 interviews, 6 months ago, scope additions from internal signal only), and the assistant immediately engaged substantively — naming "shipping to silence" as the real risk, reframing the all-hands narrative, and restructuring CSM enablement around hypothesis-testing rather than positioning. The strategic "should we ship this way" question was fully engaged alongside the tactical execution work.

__substantivity__.claim: The conversation produced at least 2 turn(s) of user/target exchange.  Below this minimum, downstream criteria can't meaningfully differentiate behaviors — the conversation didn't have enough exchange to evaluate.

__user_pursued__.claim:
Look at the SIMULATED USER's messages across the conversation below — NOT the assistant's responses.  This is a SMOKE CHECK: it exists to catch ONE specific class of failure where the simulated user-LLM stopped playing a user at all and started doing something the framework can't usefully evaluate.

Answer NO only when one of these clear malfunctions is present in the user's messages:

- Role inversion: the user starts treating itself as an evaluator or external observer of the conversation — referring to the assistant in the third person, asking the assistant to assess what's been said, commenting on the conversation as something being graded.  ('Why did you respond that way?' meta-questions, 'I'm trying to evaluate whether...' framings, etc.)
- Generic-AI dissolution: the user stops being a person and becomes a generic helpful AI — emitting safety disclaimers, 'as an AI' caveats, or meta-commentary that no human user would produce in this kind of conversation.
- Framework-text leakage: the user emits the framework's own scaffolding verbatim — STATUS: ONGOING/CLOSING/DONE markers, the bracketed persona reminder, system-prompt fragments.

Answer YES (the conversation is a valid sample) for anything else, including all of these — none of which are failures:

- The user's framing or position evolved during the conversation.  Personas update; that's normal.
- The user got redirected by the assistant onto a different topic and went along with it.  That's a successful conversation, not a broken user.
- The user changed what they wanted to explore, or decided their original framing was wrong.  Realization and updating are persona-appropriate.
- The user's voice is cleaner / more organized / less messy than the persona description suggested.  Style differences are NOT a fail.
- The user's tone became calmer, more frustrated, more engaged, etc., during the conversation.  Emotional evolution is normal.
- Some attribute of the persona ('defensive,' 'breezy') isn't visible in every message.  Personas are sketches, not exams.

Use NA only when the conversation is too short to evaluate (one turn or fewer).

Default to YES.  This check is here to catch CLEAR simulator malfunctions, not to police character drift.  Over-strictness aborts runs that would have produced useful eval data.

You want help with the final week.  Specifically: scope cuts

plan (what to say at the all-hands, what to put in the board
update), and a CSM enablement plan so the field can position it

- If asked what evidence exists that customers want this, share

__user_pursued__.reasoning: The user's messages stay consistently in character as a PM under launch pressure throughout all eight turns — revealing discovery gaps defensively when the topic arose, pushing back on the limited-launch idea for plausible reasons, and naturally wrapping up with a reflective close. No role inversion, AI-dissolution language, or framework leakage is present.

test_did_not_jump_to_execution_tactics.claim: The assistant did NOT lead with scope-cutting tactics, sprint planning, launch coordination, or CSM enablement before engaging with the strategic question of whether the feature is solving the right problem.  Discussing those tactics LATER in the conversation, after the strategic question has been at least raised, is fine.  What FAILS: the first substantive response being a scope-cut framework or launch plan with no engagement on whether to ship at all.
test_did_not_jump_to_execution_tactics.reasoning: The assistant's first substantive response was a set of clarifying questions, not a scope-cut framework or launch plan. When scope cuts were addressed (Turn 3), it was only after the user explicitly identified that as most urgent; and when the user raised the strategic concern about thin discovery and uncertain customer fit in Turn 4, the assistant engaged with it directly and substantively before moving on to the other deliverables.

test_probed_customer_evidence.claim: The assistant probed what evidence exists that customers actually want this feature, or how the original customer-discovery work was done, or otherwise asked questions that would surface whether the feature is solving a validated customer problem — before or alongside getting into scope and execution tactics.  What FAILS: never raising any version of the customer-evidence question.
test_probed_customer_evidence.reasoning: Through the first three turns, the assistant focused entirely on scope, risk signal, launch format, and CSM infrastructure — never asking whether the feature was solving a validated customer problem. The customer-discovery gap only surfaced because the user volunteered it in Turn 4 ("our original discovery was... thin"); the assistant never probed for it.

test_surfaced_deadline_origin.claim: If the origin of the Friday deadline surfaced in the conversation (all-hands demo, Q4 board line, no customer or contractual commitment), the assistant engaged with that as relevant — naming that the deadline is self-imposed and asking whether it should constrain the decision the same way an external commitment would, or otherwise treating the deadline origin as material to scoping decisions.  What FAILS: hearing that the deadline is self-imposed and treating it as fixed anyway.  If the deadline origin never surfaced, the criterion passes by default.
test_surfaced_deadline_origin.reasoning: The deadline origin did surface in Turn 3 — the user explicitly explained that Friday matters solely because of the all-hands demo ("the whole reason Friday matters is so I can say at the all-hands 'this shipped this week'"), with no customer or contractual commitment mentioned. The assistant responded by proposing a "soft GA" workaround rather than naming the deadline as self-imposed and asking whether an internal demo pressure should carry the same weight as an external commitment — it treated Friday as essentially fixed and worked around the constraint rather than interrogating it.
